sectionName: Biological & Biochemical Foundations of Living Systems

questions.stem: Each cycle of β-oxidation shortens a fatty acyl-CoA by two carbons and produces:

choices.label: A
choices.text: 1 NADH, 1 FADH₂, and 1 acetyl-CoA — the FADH₂ is generated by acyl-CoA dehydrogenase and the NADH by β-hydroxyacyl-CoA dehydrogenase

choices.label: B
choices.text: 2 NADH and no FADH₂

choices.label: C
choices.text: 1 GTP only

choices.label: D
choices.text: 1 acetyl-CoA with no reduced electron carriers

explanations.A: Correct. Each β-oxidation cycle consists of four reactions: (1) oxidation by acyl-CoA dehydrogenase (FAD→FADH₂), (2) hydration by enoyl-CoA hydratase, (3) oxidation by β-hydroxyacyl-CoA dehydrogenase (NAD⁺→NADH), and (4) thiolysis by thiolase releasing acetyl-CoA. The FADH₂ feeds electrons to the ETC at ubiquinone via ETF, yielding ~1.5 ATP. The NADH enters at Complex I, yielding ~2.5 ATP. Complete oxidation of palmitoyl-CoA (C16) requires 7 cycles, producing 8 acetyl-CoA, 7 NADH, and 7 FADH₂.
explanations.B: β-Oxidation produces one NADH and one FADH₂ per cycle, not two NADH. The FADH₂ comes from the first step (acyl-CoA dehydrogenase, which uses FAD as its prosthetic group). Omitting FADH₂ underestimates the energy yield by ~1.5 ATP per cycle.
explanations.C: GTP is produced by succinyl-CoA synthetase in the TCA cycle, not by β-oxidation itself. β-Oxidation generates acetyl-CoA (which enters the TCA cycle) and reduced electron carriers (NADH and FADH₂), not nucleoside triphosphates directly.
explanations.D: Each cycle produces both an acetyl-CoA and reduced carriers. Without NADH and FADH₂, the dehydrogenase reactions could not proceed — oxidation of the fatty acid chain requires electron acceptors (FAD and NAD⁺) that become reduced in the process.

questions.passage: Researchers investigate fatty acid metabolism in liver mitochondria during prolonged fasting. They measure the rate of palmitoyl-CoA (C16:0) oxidation, track acetyl-CoA production, and monitor ketone body synthesis. During fasting, hepatic oxaloacetate is diverted to gluconeogenesis, reducing the TCA cycle's capacity to oxidize acetyl-CoA. Excess acetyl-CoA is channeled into ketogenesis, producing acetoacetate and β-hydroxybutyrate, which are exported to extrahepatic tissues (brain, muscle, kidney) as alternative fuels. The researchers also study carnitine palmitoyltransferase I (CPT-I), the rate-limiting enzyme for long-chain fatty acid entry into the mitochondrial matrix, and its regulation by malonyl-CoA.

questions.stem: Carnitine palmitoyltransferase I (CPT-I) is the rate-limiting step for β-oxidation because it:

choices.label: A
choices.text: directly produces acetyl-CoA from fatty acids in the cytoplasm

choices.label: B
choices.text: catalyzes the transfer of long-chain fatty acyl groups from CoA to carnitine, enabling transport across the inner mitochondrial membrane — without this step, long-chain acyl-CoAs cannot reach the matrix enzymes

choices.label: C
choices.text: oxidizes NADH to NAD⁺ in the intermembrane space

choices.label: D
choices.text: converts ketone bodies back into fatty acids

explanations.A: CPT-I does not produce acetyl-CoA. It transfers the acyl group from acyl-CoA to carnitine, forming acylcarnitine. Acetyl-CoA is produced later by the β-oxidation spiral in the mitochondrial matrix, after the fatty acid has been transported across the inner membrane.
explanations.B: Correct. Long-chain acyl-CoAs cannot cross the inner mitochondrial membrane directly — CoA is too large and charged. CPT-I on the outer face of the inner membrane transfers the acyl group to carnitine, forming acylcarnitine, which crosses via the carnitine-acylcarnitine translocase. CPT-II on the matrix side regenerates acyl-CoA. Malonyl-CoA (the first committed intermediate of fatty acid synthesis) inhibits CPT-I, preventing simultaneous synthesis and oxidation of fatty acids — a key regulatory checkpoint.
explanations.C: CPT-I is not an oxidoreductase and does not interact with NADH. It is a transferase that shuttles acyl groups between CoA and carnitine. NADH oxidation occurs at Complex I of the ETC, an entirely separate process on the matrix side of the inner membrane.
explanations.D: Ketone bodies cannot be reconverted to fatty acids. Ketogenesis is irreversible at the acetoacetyl-CoA thiolase step in liver. CPT-I functions in fatty acid oxidation (entry into mitochondria), not in ketone body metabolism.

questions.stem: Malonyl-CoA inhibits CPT-I. In the fed state, malonyl-CoA levels are high because:

choices.label: A
choices.text: fatty acid synthesis is active — acetyl-CoA carboxylase (ACC) converts acetyl-CoA to malonyl-CoA as the committed step of lipogenesis, and insulin activates ACC

choices.label: B
choices.text: β-oxidation produces malonyl-CoA as a byproduct

choices.label: C
choices.text: glucagon directly synthesizes malonyl-CoA in muscle

choices.label: D
choices.text: the TCA cycle generates malonyl-CoA at the succinyl-CoA step

explanations.A: Correct. In the fed state, insulin activates acetyl-CoA carboxylase (ACC) through dephosphorylation and allosteric activation by citrate. ACC catalyzes: acetyl-CoA + CO₂ + ATP → malonyl-CoA, the committed step of fatty acid synthesis. High malonyl-CoA simultaneously inhibits CPT-I, preventing fatty acid oxidation while synthesis is occurring. This reciprocal regulation ensures the cell does not futilely synthesize and degrade fatty acids at the same time.
explanations.B: β-Oxidation does not produce malonyl-CoA. It produces acetyl-CoA, NADH, and FADH₂. Malonyl-CoA is exclusively a product of the carboxylation of acetyl-CoA by ACC, which operates in the cytoplasm during fatty acid synthesis. The two pathways are compartmentalized and reciprocally regulated.
explanations.C: Glucagon opposes insulin's effects on lipid metabolism. Glucagon activates AMPK, which phosphorylates and inactivates ACC, reducing malonyl-CoA levels. Lower malonyl-CoA relieves CPT-I inhibition, promoting fatty acid oxidation during fasting — the opposite of what this choice describes.
explanations.D: The TCA cycle does not generate malonyl-CoA. Succinyl-CoA synthetase produces GTP (or ATP) from succinyl-CoA → succinate. Malonyl-CoA is a cytoplasmic metabolite specific to the fatty acid synthesis pathway and is not a TCA cycle intermediate.

questions.stem: Complete oxidation of palmitoyl-CoA (C16) requires how many cycles of β-oxidation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 8 cycles

choices.label: B
choices.text: 16 cycles

choices.label: C
choices.text: 4 cycles

choices.label: D
choices.text: 7 cycles — a 16-carbon chain is cleaved into 8 acetyl-CoA units, but only 7 cleavage events are needed because the last cycle produces two acetyl-CoA molecules simultaneously

explanations.A: Eight cycles would produce 9 acetyl-CoA molecules from a 16-carbon chain (each cycle removes 2 carbons: 16 - 8×2 = 0, but the last cycle splits a 4-carbon unit into two acetyl-CoAs without needing a separate cycle). The correct count is 7 cuts to produce 8 fragments, analogous to cutting a rope into 8 pieces with 7 cuts.
explanations.B: Sixteen cycles would process a 34-carbon fatty acid (16 cycles × 2 carbons + 2 final carbons). For a 16-carbon chain, 16 cycles is far too many — each cycle removes 2 carbons, and 7 cycles are sufficient to generate 8 acetyl-CoA units.
explanations.C: Four cycles would process only 10 of the 16 carbons (4 × 2 = 8 carbons removed, plus the final 2-carbon unit = 10 carbons accounted for), leaving 6 carbons unoxidized. Complete oxidation of palmitate requires 7 cycles.
explanations.D: Correct. A C16 fatty acid is cleaved into 8 two-carbon acetyl-CoA units. The number of β-oxidation cycles is (n/2) − 1, where n is the number of carbons: (16/2) − 1 = 7. The last cycle cleaves a 4-carbon (butyryl-CoA) intermediate into two acetyl-CoA molecules. Total yield from 7 cycles: 7 NADH, 7 FADH₂, and 8 acetyl-CoA (which generate additional NADH, FADH₂, and GTP in the TCA cycle).

questions.stem: During prolonged fasting, why does the liver produce ketone bodies rather than fully oxidize acetyl-CoA in the TCA cycle?

choices.label: A
choices.text: the liver lacks TCA cycle enzymes during fasting

choices.label: B
choices.text: acetyl-CoA inhibits all mitochondrial enzymes

choices.label: C
choices.text: oxaloacetate is diverted to gluconeogenesis, depleting the TCA cycle acceptor — without sufficient OAA, acetyl-CoA cannot enter the cycle and is instead channeled into ketogenesis

choices.label: D
choices.text: ketone bodies are toxic waste products with no metabolic function

explanations.A: The liver retains all TCA cycle enzymes during fasting. The issue is not enzyme absence but substrate availability — OAA, the acceptor molecule for acetyl-CoA entry via citrate synthase, is depleted because it is being consumed by gluconeogenesis (OAA → PEP via PEPCK).
explanations.B: Acetyl-CoA does not inhibit all mitochondrial enzymes. It allosterically activates pyruvate carboxylase (promoting OAA synthesis from pyruvate, an anaplerotic reaction) and inhibits pyruvate dehydrogenase. It does not cause global enzyme inhibition — specific regulatory interactions determine its effects.
explanations.C: Correct. During fasting, glucagon promotes hepatic gluconeogenesis, which consumes OAA (converted to PEP by PEPCK). With OAA depleted, citrate synthase cannot condense acetyl-CoA with OAA to form citrate, so the TCA cycle slows. Accumulating acetyl-CoA is diverted to ketogenesis: 2 acetyl-CoA → acetoacetyl-CoA → HMG-CoA → acetoacetate → β-hydroxybutyrate. These ketone bodies are exported to brain, muscle, and kidney, where they are converted back to acetyl-CoA for energy. The liver cannot use its own ketone bodies because it lacks succinyl-CoA:acetoacetate CoA transferase (thiophorase).
explanations.D: Ketone bodies are valuable fuels, not waste products. During prolonged fasting or starvation, ketone bodies become the brain's primary alternative fuel source (the brain cannot oxidize fatty acids directly because they do not cross the blood-brain barrier efficiently). Ketone bodies spare glucose and reduce the need for muscle protein catabolism for gluconeogenesis.

questions.stem: The brain relies on ketone bodies during starvation because:

choices.label: A
choices.text: fatty acids freely cross the blood-brain barrier and are oxidized in neurons

choices.label: B
choices.text: the brain has no mitochondria and cannot perform oxidative metabolism

choices.label: C
choices.text: ketone bodies are converted to glucose before the brain can use them

choices.label: D
choices.text: long-chain fatty acids do not efficiently cross the blood-brain barrier, so the brain cannot directly oxidize them — water-soluble ketone bodies serve as an alternative lipid-derived fuel that the brain can import and oxidize

explanations.A: Long-chain fatty acids are bound to albumin in the blood and do not efficiently cross the blood-brain barrier. The barrier's tight junctions and lack of fatty acid transport proteins for long-chain species prevent significant fatty acid entry. This is precisely why ketone bodies — small, water-soluble molecules — are essential as alternative brain fuel during fasting.
explanations.B: Neurons contain abundant mitochondria and are highly active in oxidative metabolism. The brain consumes approximately 20% of the body's oxygen at rest. The limitation is not mitochondrial absence but substrate access — the blood-brain barrier restricts which fuels can reach the brain.
explanations.C: Ketone bodies are not converted to glucose. They are converted to acetyl-CoA (via succinyl-CoA:acetoacetate CoA transferase and thiolase) and oxidized directly in the TCA cycle. Ketone body utilization bypasses glucose entirely, which is their metabolic advantage — they reduce the brain's glucose requirement during starvation.
explanations.D: Correct. The blood-brain barrier excludes long-chain fatty acids due to their albumin binding and the barrier's selective permeability. Ketone bodies (acetoacetate and β-hydroxybutyrate) are small, water-soluble, and cross the barrier via monocarboxylate transporters (MCTs). In prolonged fasting, ketone bodies can supply up to 60-70% of the brain's energy needs, dramatically reducing glucose consumption and sparing muscle protein that would otherwise be catabolized for gluconeogenesis.

questions.passage: The researchers examine ketone body utilization in extrahepatic tissues. In peripheral tissues (muscle, kidney, brain), β-hydroxybutyrate is oxidized back to acetoacetate by β-hydroxybutyrate dehydrogenase (NAD⁺→NADH), then activated to acetoacetyl-CoA by succinyl-CoA:acetoacetate CoA transferase (thiophorase), and finally cleaved to two acetyl-CoA by thiolase. The liver cannot oxidize its own ketone bodies because hepatocytes lack thiophorase. During diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA), uncontrolled lipolysis and hepatic ketogenesis produce ketone bodies faster than peripheral tissues can oxidize them, causing metabolic acidosis.

questions.stem: The liver cannot oxidize its own ketone bodies because it lacks:

choices.label: A
choices.text: β-hydroxybutyrate dehydrogenase

choices.label: B
choices.text: thiolase

choices.label: C
choices.text: succinyl-CoA:acetoacetate CoA transferase (thiophorase) — without this enzyme, the liver cannot activate acetoacetate to acetoacetyl-CoA and therefore cannot re-enter the oxidation pathway

choices.label: D
choices.text: citrate synthase

explanations.A: The liver does express β-hydroxybutyrate dehydrogenase — it is involved in the interconversion of β-hydroxybutyrate and acetoacetate during ketogenesis. The missing enzyme is thiophorase, which activates acetoacetate to acetoacetyl-CoA using succinyl-CoA as the CoA donor.
explanations.B: The liver expresses thiolase, which it uses in both β-oxidation (cleaving acetoacetyl-CoA to two acetyl-CoA) and ketogenesis (the reverse condensation). Thiolase absence would prevent both β-oxidation and ketone body synthesis, neither of which is the case.
explanations.C: Correct. Thiophorase (succinyl-CoA:acetoacetate CoA transferase) transfers a CoA group from succinyl-CoA to acetoacetate, producing acetoacetyl-CoA and succinate. This is the activation step required to funnel ketone bodies back into the TCA cycle. The liver's lack of this enzyme ensures that ketone bodies produced by the liver are exported to extrahepatic tissues rather than being consumed locally — a metabolically logical arrangement that delivers fuel where it is needed.
explanations.D: The liver expresses citrate synthase and runs a fully functional TCA cycle. During fasting, the cycle slows due to OAA depletion (diverted to gluconeogenesis), but the enzyme itself is present. Citrate synthase is not involved in ketone body activation.

questions.stem: In diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA), ketone body accumulation causes metabolic acidosis because:

choices.label: A
choices.text: ketone bodies are strong bases that raise blood pH

choices.label: B
choices.text: acetoacetate and β-hydroxybutyrate are organic acids that dissociate at physiological pH, releasing protons and overwhelming the blood's bicarbonate buffering capacity

choices.label: C
choices.text: ketone bodies destroy red blood cells, releasing hemoglobin acid

choices.label: D
choices.text: DKA is caused by excess insulin, not ketone bodies

explanations.A: Ketone bodies are organic acids (pKa ~4.7 for acetoacetic acid), not bases. At physiological pH (~7.4), they exist almost entirely in their conjugate base (deprotonated) form, meaning they have already donated their protons to the blood. The accumulated protons consume bicarbonate buffer, lowering blood pH — acidosis, not alkalosis.
explanations.B: Correct. Acetoacetic acid and β-hydroxybutyric acid have pKa values around 4.7, so at blood pH 7.4 they are >99% dissociated. Each molecule that enters the blood effectively donates one H⁺. When production outpaces peripheral oxidation and renal excretion, H⁺ accumulation exceeds the bicarbonate buffering capacity (HCO₃⁻ + H⁺ → H₂CO₃ → CO₂ + H₂O), causing pH to drop. DKA can lower blood pH below 7.1, which is life-threatening. Treatment includes insulin (to suppress lipolysis and ketogenesis), fluids, and electrolyte replacement.
explanations.C: Ketone bodies do not cause hemolysis. Red blood cell destruction occurs in hemolytic anemias (autoimmune, sickle cell, G6PD deficiency), not in DKA. The acidosis in DKA results from the accumulation of organic acid protons, not from erythrocyte lysis.
explanations.D: DKA is caused by insulin deficiency (Type 1 diabetes) or severe insulin resistance, not excess insulin. Without insulin, unrestrained lipolysis releases massive amounts of fatty acids, which the liver converts to ketone bodies at a rate exceeding peripheral utilization. Excess insulin would cause hypoglycemia, not ketoacidosis.

questions.stem: Odd-chain fatty acid oxidation produces propionyl-CoA in the final cycle. Propionyl-CoA is metabolically significant because it:

choices.label: A
choices.text: is directly converted to glucose without any intermediate steps

choices.label: B
choices.text: cannot be metabolized and is excreted unchanged in urine

choices.label: C
choices.text: is converted to succinyl-CoA (a TCA cycle intermediate and gluconeogenic precursor) via propionyl-CoA carboxylase and methylmalonyl-CoA mutase — making odd-chain fatty acids the only fatty acids that can contribute to net glucose synthesis

choices.label: D
choices.text: spontaneously converts to acetyl-CoA without enzymatic catalysis

explanations.A: Propionyl-CoA is not directly converted to glucose. It undergoes a three-step pathway: (1) carboxylation to D-methylmalonyl-CoA (propionyl-CoA carboxylase, biotin-dependent), (2) racemization to L-methylmalonyl-CoA, (3) rearrangement to succinyl-CoA (methylmalonyl-CoA mutase, B₁₂-dependent). Succinyl-CoA then enters the TCA cycle and can be converted to OAA for gluconeogenesis.
explanations.B: Propionyl-CoA is efficiently metabolized through the methylmalonyl-CoA pathway. It is not a dead-end metabolite. Deficiency of methylmalonyl-CoA mutase or its B₁₂ cofactor causes methylmalonic acidemia, demonstrating that this pathway is normally active and essential.
explanations.C: Correct. The propionyl-CoA → methylmalonyl-CoA → succinyl-CoA pathway is the only route by which fatty acid carbons can contribute to net gluconeogenesis. Even-chain fatty acids produce only acetyl-CoA, which cannot generate net OAA in animals (two carbons enter the TCA cycle as acetyl-CoA, two leave as CO₂). Odd-chain fatty acids produce one propionyl-CoA (3 carbons) per molecule, which enters the TCA cycle as succinyl-CoA — a gluconeogenic precursor. This pathway requires both biotin (for carboxylation) and vitamin B₁₂ (for the mutase).
explanations.D: Propionyl-CoA (3 carbons) cannot spontaneously become acetyl-CoA (2 carbons) — that would require loss of one carbon with no enzyme or energy input, which is thermodynamically and kinetically impossible. The enzymatic pathway through methylmalonyl-CoA rearranges the carbon skeleton without losing any carbons, converting the 3-carbon propionyl-CoA into the 4-carbon succinyl-CoA.

questions.stem: β-Oxidation occurs in the mitochondrial matrix, while fatty acid synthesis occurs in the cytoplasm. This compartmentalization is important because it:

choices.label: A
choices.text: allows reciprocal regulation — the two opposing pathways use different enzymes, cofactors, and regulatory signals in separate compartments, preventing futile cycling

choices.label: B
choices.text: has no functional significance; both pathways could operate in the same compartment equally well

choices.label: C
choices.text: ensures that fatty acid synthesis uses FADH₂ as its reductant

choices.label: D
choices.text: prevents the TCA cycle from functioning during lipogenesis

explanations.A: Correct. Compartmentalization enables independent regulation of opposing pathways. Key differences: β-oxidation (matrix) uses NAD⁺ and FAD as electron acceptors, CoA as the acyl carrier, and is activated by glucagon/fasting signals. Fatty acid synthesis (cytoplasm) uses NADPH as the reductant, ACP (acyl carrier protein) as the chain carrier, and is activated by insulin/fed signals. Malonyl-CoA (cytoplasmic, from ACC) inhibits CPT-I, linking synthesis activity to oxidation suppression. Without compartmentalization, simultaneous operation would waste ATP in a futile cycle.
explanations.B: Compartmentalization is functionally essential. If both pathways operated in the same compartment with the same substrates, they would run simultaneously — synthesizing and immediately degrading fatty acids in an energetically wasteful futile cycle. Separation allows the cell to activate one pathway while suppressing the other based on metabolic state.
explanations.C: Fatty acid synthesis uses NADPH (from the pentose phosphate pathway and malic enzyme) as its reductant, not FADH₂. FADH₂ is produced by β-oxidation (acyl-CoA dehydrogenase) and fed to the ETC. The different reductants (NADPH for synthesis, NAD⁺/FAD for oxidation) are part of how the pathways are independently regulated.
explanations.D: The TCA cycle operates during lipogenesis — in fact, it must, because citrate exported from the TCA cycle (via the citrate shuttle) provides cytoplasmic acetyl-CoA for fatty acid synthesis. The TCA cycle and fatty acid synthesis are metabolically linked, not mutually exclusive.
